ENGNew: What Became of the Three Delinquents
changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "Details of the Delinquent Three"

TextENGNew: ``Why the hell are we gettin’ arrested?!!``
``This is bullshit!! Let go of me!!!``
``Kuma-chan, you can let go of their arms now. Come, come, please have a seat.``
Kumagai-kun and the others must have been squeezing their arms quite hard—the three of them were rubbing their arms in pain.
``Nahahaha... Don’t misunderstand, gentlemen.@k@vS20/11/440700063. You haven’t been arrested or anything.``
``Kuma-chan, grab something cold from the fridge, would you?@k@vS20/11/440700065. Gentlemen, would you prefer your barley tea with bubbles, or without?@k@vS20/11/440700066. Hmm?``
They dropped heavily onto the reception sofa. They’d stopped shouting, but their expressions remained fierce.@k...Ah, to be young again.
``If you’re not going to drink, I’ll help myself. Excuse me—psshht.@k@vS20/11/440700068. ...Mmm~~~!!!@k@vS20/11/440700069. Nothing beats a beer on the clock~!``
After watching him enjoy his beer, the three exchanged glances and finally reached for their cans.
...Their guard was still up, but they couldn’t find a reason to refuse.
Each of them cracked open a can and started drinking.
``So where are you gentlemen from? You’re not from around here.@k@vS20/11/440700071. Nfufufufu!``
They exchanged suspicious glances, unsure whether to answer.
``College students, are you? Friends on a trip together?@k@vS20/11/440700073. Probably touring around on motorcycles.@k@vS20/11/440700074. How I envy you!``
``You’re not from nearby, are you? Where did you come from?@k@vS20/11/440700076. Osaka, perhaps?``
``Who cares where we’re from?``
``Now, now, no need to be hostile. If I’d left you there, you’d have been in serious trouble, right?``
They seemed to be remembering the harrowing experience from earlier.
changeReason: Japanese 刺激の強かった describes an intensely stimulating event; old's 'thrilling' implies positive excitement, while new's 'harrowing' implies distressing, which better fits the context of being surrounded by hostile villagers.

TextENGNew: ...Getting surrounded by other students or a biker gang—that they could understand.
...But being encircled by an entire community of locals—young and old, men and women alike—that was something else entirely.
It was easy to imagine just how terrifying that must have been.
"Anyone from around here would never lay a hand on a Hinamizawa person, no matter what kind of mistake they made. ......The folks over there are special."
changeReason: Old reverses the meaning: says Hinamizawa people wouldn't do anything to outsiders, but JPN says outsiders would never touch Hinamizawa people. New correctly captures that locals fear/respect Hinamizawa.

TextENGNew: ``It’s not the kind of thing where parents stay out of their children’s fights. ...If you mess with one of them, the entire village comes out in force.@k@vS20/11/440700080. And I’m not just saying that to scare you.``
No rebuttal.
...They’d seen it with their own eyes. They’d been the ones surrounded. There was no room for doubt.
``Around here... if the people of Hinamizawa set their sights on you, you’ll simply be “demoned away” and disappear.@k@vS20/11/440700082. Suddenly. Without a trace.``

``...Do any of you have family members who would file a missing persons report if you vanished? If not, that’s a problem.@k@vS20/11/440700084. You’d just be written off as runaways.@k@vS20/11/440700085. Nfufufufu!``
changeReason: Japanese 蒸発扱い means being classified as having voluntarily disappeared/evaporated; old's 'vanish, and that'll be the end of it' misses the specific classification, while new's 'written off as runaways' better conveys the official dismissal.

TextENGNew: We’d arrived just in time, so nothing happened. But if we’d been even a little later, things could have gotten ugly.
If they’d been dragged into an alley... these young men might not be sitting on a sofa right now—they might be lying on an autopsy table.@k...Nfufufu! Well, maybe not...
Anyway.@k...At least when there’s a body to find, that’s still the tamer outcome.
changeReason: Old mistranslates the Japanese entirely: it says the delinquents would be 'more cooperative as corpses.' The Japanese actually means 'at least finding a body is still the tamer outcome' (implying sometimes bodies aren't even found).

TextENGNew: ``...How did you gentlemen get to Okinomiya? By train?@k@vS20/11/440700087. Motorcycle?``
``That’s a problem. They’re still parked back there, aren’t they?@k@vS20/11/440700089. If you waltz back to retrieve them, you’ll really get beaten to a pulp this time.``
``Wh-... What the hell!! Bring it on!!!``
changeReason: Old contains garbled text 'Hellsat?' which appears to be an error; new correctly translates the Japanese outburst as 'What the hell!! Bring it on!!!'

TextENGNew: Calm down, calm down...
Why do young people these days have to yell about everything...?

``You’ve had enough trouble for one day, haven’t you? Just give me your license plate numbers.@k@vS20/11/440700091. I’ll have the bikes brought here for you.@k@vS20/11/440700092. ...Kuma-chan, go borrow the flatbed from the traffic division.``
changeReason: Old translates ゲート車 as 'loading truck we use for traffic disasters'; new correctly uses 'flatbed' and translates 交通課 as 'traffic division' rather than 'traffic disasters.'

TextENGNew: ``Yes, sir!``
``N-Nah, it’s fine...! We’ll go get ’em ourselves!``
``Do you honestly want to get surrounded again? The police might not make it in time next time.``
``It’s fine, I said! Let ’em surround us! We’ll take ’em!!``

``Hey. Gentlemen.@k@vS20/11/440700095. You just drank the barley tea with bubbles, didn’t you?@k@vS20/11/440700096. ...Go ahead and start your engines.@k@vS20/11/440700097. I’ll cite you for drunk driving on the spot.``
``...You... that’s dirty...!!``
``The police are offering to transport your bikes back here at taxpayer expense. Quit your bellyaching and give me the damn plate numbers, you little punks.``
The three were reluctant to share their plate numbers.
...They mumbled excuses about having just bought the bikes and not remembering them yet.
...Well, I figured as much. Sigh...

``Kuma-chan. Go along with the flatbed and bring the bikes back.@k@vS20/11/440700100. There’s a beefy red one, one with the right tail light cracked,@k@vS20/11/440700101. and a white one with duct tape on the seat.@k@vS20/11/440700102. Those are the ones, right?@k@vS20/11/440700103. They’re yours, gentlemen.``
No reply—but from the looks of it, I’d hit the mark.
``Ooishi-san, the flatbed’s here. I’m heading out.``
``Right, right, thanks. Oh, Kuma-chan, one more thing.``
``What’s up?``
``Run the plate numbers while you’re at it. Wouldn’t want to bring back the wrong bikes.``
The color drained from all three faces.
...These kids really were easy to read...

``Let’s just relax here until your bikes arrive. There’s plenty more beer.@k@vS20/11/440700107. We’ve got snacks, too.@k@vS20/11/440700108. Want to watch TV?@k@vS20/11/440700109. Nothing good’s on, though.``
``Nah, we’re outta here. We don’t wanna hang around this place!``
Without anyone noticing, five or six burly officers had gathered around them.
changeReason: Japanese いつの間にか means 'without anyone noticing/before they realized'; old uses 'Suddenly' which implies abruptness, while new's 'Without anyone noticing' correctly conveys the stealthy gathering.

TextENGNew: Their unmistakably intimidating stares pinned the three to the sofa.
``...Wha-... What?!! What did we do?!!``
Riding stolen motorcycles on an extortion tour.@k...Students with too much time on their hands.
changeReason: Japanese 恐喝行脚 means 'extortion tour/pilgrimage'; old translates only 'going on a trip' and omits the extortion, while new correctly includes 'extortion tour.'
